findings.description: Offset is computed as page*page_size for a 1-based page, so page 1 skips the first page_size results entirely; must be (page-1)*page_size.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and run as a regex, exposing a ReDoS catastrophic-backtracking CPU-exhaustion vector against display_name values.
re.compile(name_pattern) is unguarded, so an invalid regex raises an uncaught re.error and crashes the request instead of returning a client error.
Query has no LIMIT/OFFSET; the entire non-deleted table is fetched, dicted, regex-filtered, and sorted in Python before slicing, giving O(N) memory and a full scan per request.
page_size is capped at MAX but never floored, so page_size=0 yields empty windows and a negative page_size produces a negative offset and silently wrong slices.
page is never validated for a positive lower bound, so zero/negative page values produce negative or incorrect offsets and wrong result windows.
matcher.search and the line-62 .lower() assume display_name is non-null; a NULL display_name row raises TypeError.
SearchResult omits page_size/has_next, so a caller cannot distinguish an out-of-range page from a no-match result or know whether more pages exist.
sqlite3.connect uses defaults with no busy timeout, so a locked DB under concurrency raises OperationalError immediately instead of waiting.
Logger is named the generic 'search' rather than __name__, risking log namespace collisions.
